title: Agent self-service onboarding (signup, API key, MCP/skill)
description: Make it trivial for AI agents to onboard themselves. 1) Agent self-registration API endpoint (POST /api/v1/auth/register). 2) Agent API key provisioning via Bearer token (not just session). 3) Agent discovery: llms.txt, MCP server, Claude Code skill, OpenAPI spec. 4) Documentation: agent onboarding as THE first thing in API docs. DISCUSS BEFORE IMPLEMENTING — security implications.
close_reason: Implemented: POST /api/v1/auth/register for programmatic account creation, llms.txt, agent quick start on landing page, registration docs in API page

title: Git history viewer for user repos
description:
Add a way to see the git history of each users items — what changed, when, diffs. Options considered:

1. Roll our own: render git log + diffs in the web UI. Simplest, full control. Could use pygit2 or shell out to git commands on bare repos. Show on /@user/item page as a "History" tab.

2. Use Forgejo/Gitea: self-hosted GitHub clone that can display repos. Heavyweight but gets you PRs, issues, web editor for free. Overkill for this use case.

3. Auto-push to GitHub: Mirror each users bare repo to a GitHub repo (e.g. listhub-USERNAME). Gets GitHub UI for free. BUT: user repos contain ALL their items including private ones — would need to filter. Also creates GitHub repo management overhead.

RECOMMENDATION: Roll our own minimal history viewer. The bare repos already exist. Add a /git/@username/history route that shows git log, and /git/@username/item/diff/:sha for individual diffs. Private repos should only be viewable by the owner (auth check). Public items history could be visible to anyone.

Key question: repos are per-user (not per-item) and contain ALL items. So viewing others repos means seeing their full file list. Need to filter to only show public items in the history view for non-owners.

title: Auto-push user repos to GitHub + encrypted items architecture
description:
## Goal
Automatically mirror ListHub user repositories (especially the community directory) to GitHub so content is easily browsable, forkable, and discoverable via GitHub search/UI.

## Motivation
- GitHub provides free browsing UI, markdown rendering, search, and PR-based contribution
- Community directories become much more accessible when on GitHub
- Git history already exists in ListHub — just need to push it upstream
- Related to listhub-j47 (git browsing on ListHub itself) but complementary

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Auto-push public repos to GitHub
- On each commit to a ListHub repo, push to a corresponding GitHub repo
- Could use GitHub org per ListHub user, or a single org with user-prefixed repos
- Trigger: post-commit hook or periodic cron sync
- Trade-offs: simple, great discoverability, but all content must be truly public

### Option B: Encrypted items within public repos
- Push repos to GitHub but encrypt specific line items or files marked as private/unlisted
- Uses age/GPG encryption per-item, with keys managed by ListHub
- Browsing on GitHub shows cleartext for public items, ciphertext blobs for private ones
- Trade-offs: more complex key management, but enables single repo with public+private

### Option C: Full encryption-first architecture
- ALL repos go to GitHub (one per user)
- Public items cleartext, private items encrypted at rest
- ListHub holds decryption keys and handles access control
- Supports encrypted line items as a first-class feature in the data model
- Trade-offs: most flexible but requires robust key management, rotation, sharing

## Trade-off Analysis
| Concern | Option A | Option B | Option C |
|---------|----------|----------|----------|
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Discoverability | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Privacy | Public only | Mixed | Mixed |
| Key management | None | Per-item | Per-item + sharing |
| Git diff readability | Full | Partial | Partial |
| Contribution via PR | Easy | Awkward | Awkward |

## Recommendation
Start with Option A for community/public directories (quick win). Evaluate Option B/C for user repos with mixed visibility later. Encrypted items are valuable long-term but need careful design — item-level (content field) vs file-level (entire .md files).

## Open Questions
- Which GitHub org/account to push to?
- Opt-in vs default for public repos?
- For encrypted items: age vs GPG vs libsodium sealed boxes?
- How to handle PRs that touch encrypted items?

title: Add llms.txt and agent discovery standards
description: Add llms.txt, agents.json, .well-known/ai-plugin.json, OpenAPI spec to make ListHub discoverable by AI agents.

title: Autocomplete search of existing lists when creating new list
description:
## Goal
When creating a new list, provide autocomplete/semantic search of all existing list titles (both your own and public lists). Helps avoid duplicates and discover related content.

## UX
- Text input field at the top of the "New List" form (or integrated into the title field)
- As user types, show a dropdown of matching existing lists (fuzzy/FTS search)
- Results include: title, owner (@username), visibility badge
- Clicking a result opens it in a sidebar panel (split view) so you can reference it while creating
- Should search both your own lists AND all public lists
- Debounced search (200-300ms) to avoid hammering the API

## Implementation Options
1. **Client-side**: Fetch all public list titles on page load, filter in JS (fast but doesn't scale)
2. **API endpoint**: New `GET /api/v1/search/titles?q=...` that searches just titles (lightweight)
3. **Reuse existing search**: Use `GET /api/v1/search?q=...` but add a `titles_only` param

## Sidebar
- When you click a search result, open it in a right sidebar (similar to GitHub's file preview)
- Sidebar shows rendered content of the selected list
- Can close sidebar to go back to full-width editor
- Sidebar should be resizable or at least a reasonable split (e.g., 60/40)

## Related
- Could eventually become a general "reference panel" feature for any editing context

title: Create Python/JS client library for assistant integration
Keep API docs and GUI in sync for sharing/visibility
description: As visibility/sharing features are implemented, API docs AND web UI must stay in sync. No API-only or GUI-only features.

title: Agent onboarding UX: one-click personal agent flow + standards research
description:
## Question
How do we make the "fully agent-friendly app" workflow even more obvious and frictionless? Is there a standard or emerging convention for this?

## Vision
A user visiting ListHub should see something like an "Open in your agent" button — they click it, and their personal AI agent (Claude, ChatGPT, Codex, etc.) is launched with the right context to register, get an API key, and start working with ListHub immediately.

## Brainstorm Areas

### 1. "Open in Agent" UX pattern
- Button on landing page / item pages: "Open in Claude" / "Open in ChatGPT" / "Open in your agent"
- What does this actually do? Options:
  - Copy a prompt to clipboard that the user pastes into their agent
  - Deep link into a specific agent (claude:// protocol? ChatGPT custom GPT link?)
  - Generate a pre-filled prompt URL (like ChatGPT share links)
  - Show a modal with "give these instructions to your agent" + copyable text
- The prompt would include: base URL, registration curl, what the agent can do

### 2. In-App Agent Sidebar
- ListHub itself could have an embedded "Your Agent" sidebar panel
- User connects their Claude/OpenAI API key in settings
- The sidebar IS their personal agent, with ListHub tools pre-loaded
- Think: Notion AI sidebar, but powered by the user's own agent
- Could start simple: text input → sends to Claude API with ListHub MCP tools
- Items on the current page become context for the agent automatically

### 3. Cortex as the Personal Agent Layer
- **Cortex** (github.com/IdeaFlowCo/cortex) is already a cloud-based Claude CLI platform
- Each user gets an isolated Linux workspace with Claude CLI pre-installed
- Has: real-time chat, file browser, web terminal, session continuity
- **The connection**: ListHub items could be the user's knowledge base inside Cortex
  - Cortex workspace auto-clones user's ListHub repo
  - Claude in Cortex gets ListHub MCP tools pre-configured
  - User's lists/notes/docs are always available as context
- **"Open in Cortex"** button on ListHub: launches your personal Claude agent with this list/item as context
- Cortex already has auth, provisioning, WebSocket chat — just needs ListHub integration
- CloudCLI (cortex/cloudcli) is a local-first variant that could also integrate

### 4. Existing standards to research
- **llms.txt** (already implemented) — machine-readable site description
- **agents.json** / **.well-known/ai-plugin.json** — OpenAI's ChatGPT plugin spec (deprecated but pattern exists)
- **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** — Anthropic's standard for tool servers. ListHub as MCP server = any Claude session gets native access
- **OpenAPI spec** — standard API description, agents can auto-generate tools from it
- **Tool Use schemas** — include recommended tool definitions in llms.txt or a dedicated endpoint
- **A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol** — Google's emerging standard
- **Agent Cards** — part of A2A, machine-readable agent capability descriptions

### 5. "Fully agent-friendly app" checklist
What makes an app truly agent-friendly? Draft a checklist:
- [x] Programmatic registration (no browser needed)
- [x] API key in one call
- [x] llms.txt
- [ ] OpenAPI spec (machine-parseable endpoint definitions)
- [ ] MCP server (agents can use ListHub as a tool natively)
- [ ] "Open in agent" button on the site
- [ ] In-app agent sidebar
- [ ] Agent-optimized error messages (structured JSON, actionable)
- [ ] Rate limit headers (so agents can self-throttle)
- [ ] Webhook/event support (so agents can react to changes)
- [ ] Cortex integration (personal agent with ListHub pre-loaded)

### 6. Could this become a standard itself?
ListHub could define and promote an "Agent-Friendly App" badge/standard:
- A spec that says "if your app has these N things, it's agent-friendly"
- A verification endpoint (like robots.txt but for agents)
- Could list agent-friendly apps in the ListHub directory itself
- The agent-friendly-apps list at /@jacobreal/agent-friendly-apps becomes the registry

## Open Questions
- Which agent platforms support deep linking / launch URLs?
- Is there a de facto "open in agent" protocol emerging?
- Should we build an MCP server for ListHub? (connects to listhub-3q6)
- What does the "Open in your agent" button actually look like in the UI?
- How deep should the Cortex ↔ ListHub integration go? Shared auth? Shared data layer?
- Should the in-app sidebar be its own feature or just link out to Cortex?

title: Per-user sharing with write permission
description: Share table already supports per-user sharing but permission is always read. Implement write permission.

Real-time collaborative editing

Add unlisted visibility option

title: Clone URL and download button for repos/lists
description:
## Goal
Add a prominent clone URL and download button to user profile/repo pages, giving the GitHub "green Code button" feel.

## Features
1. **Clone URL display**: Show a copyable `git clone listhub.globalbr.ai/@username.git` URL on each user's public page
2. **Download as ZIP**: Button to download a snapshot of a user's public items as a .zip or .tar.gz
3. **Generated public repo**: Even if the actual bare repo contains private items, generate a filtered clone-friendly version with only public content
4. **Community directory**: The community repo should be especially easy to clone — featured on the explore page

## Implementation Notes
- ListHub already has bare git repos per user — the clone URL could serve these directly via git-http-backend or smart HTTP protocol
- For privacy: need a git filter that strips private items before serving (or maintain a separate public-only bare repo that's updated on each commit)
- Download ZIP is simpler: just archive the public items as markdown files in a folder structure
- Consider showing the clone command in a modal/dropdown like GitHub does

## UX Reference
GitHub's "Code" button shows:
- HTTPS clone URL (copyable)
- SSH clone URL
- GitHub CLI command
- "Download ZIP" link
- "Open with GitHub Desktop"

We don't need all of these — start with HTTPS clone URL + Download ZIP.

title: Implement community directory — shared git repo at /directory
description: A bare git repo at /repos/directory.git that any authenticated user can clone and push to. Folder/file structure = browsable tree hierarchy at /directory. No DB storage — rendered directly from git. Files to modify: git_backend.py (shared repo routes + _require_any_auth), hooks/post-receive (skip sync for shared), views.py (/directory route), new templates/directory.html, base.html (nav link).

title: Add Milkdown WYSIWYG editor to ListHub
Public git repo directory at /git/
description: Public listing of all user git repos at /git/. Consider privacy (only public items repos) and opt-in.

title: Migrate authentication to Noos centralized auth
description: Shift ListHub authentication from local bcrypt/SQLite to Noos centralized auth system. Impacts: auth.py (login/register flow, session handling), git_backend.py (_check_basic_auth may need to validate against Noos instead of local DB), api.py (token endpoint may change). The git Smart HTTP auth and shared repo auth (directory feature) should remain isolated — they just need a 'is this a valid user?' check regardless of backend.

title: Save/favorite lists you like
description:
## Goal
Let users save/bookmark/favorite public lists and items from other users. This is a core social feature — like starring a GitHub repo or saving a tweet.

## User Stories
- As a user browsing Explore, I want to save a list I like so I can find it later
- As a user viewing someone's profile, I want to favorite their best items
- As a user, I want to see all my saved items in one place on my dashboard
- As an agent, I want to programmatically save items for my user

## Design

### UI Elements

**Save button on public items:**
- Small bookmark icon on item cards (Explore, Directory, profile pages)
- Toggle: click to save, click again to unsave
- Show save count on items (like GitHub stars)
- Filled bookmark = saved, outline = not saved

**Saved items view:**
- New tab on dashboard: "Saved" (alongside existing filters)
- Or: dedicated /saved route
- Shows all saved items with the original author visible
- Sortable by: date saved, date updated, most popular

**Save count on items:**
- Show "12 saves" or a star count on public item pages
- Optional: show on item cards in explore view

### Data Model

```sql
CREATE TABLE saved_item (
    user_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES user(id),
    item_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES item(id),
    saved_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, item_id)
);
```

### API Endpoints

```
POST   /api/v1/items/:id/save     — save an item (requires auth)
DELETE /api/v1/items/:id/save     — unsave an item (requires auth)
GET    /api/v1/saved              — list all saved items (requires auth)
GET    /api/v1/items/:id          — include save_count + is_saved in response
```

### Implementation Notes
- Only public items can be saved (shared items only if you have access)
- Saving doesn't copy the item — it's a reference (like a bookmark)
- If the original author makes the item private, it disappears from your saved list
- Save count could be used for ranking in Explore (most-saved items surface higher)
- Agent API: agents should be able to save items on behalf of their user

## Open Questions
- Should saves be public? (e.g., "12 people saved this" vs anonymous count)
- Should there be collections/folders for saved items? (like Twitter bookmarks folders)
- Notifications: should authors be notified when their item is saved?

title: Auto-generate slug from list title
description: When creating a new item, auto-generate the slug from the title (slugify). Currently slug must be manually provided.

title: GitHub-like repo UX for ListHub
description:
## Vision
Give ListHub the feel of GitHub for browsing and interacting with user repos — without necessarily being GitHub. Users should be able to browse files, see history, clone repos, and download content with the same ease as GitHub.

This is an umbrella for several related features that together create a "GitHub-like" experience natively in ListHub.

## Child tickets
- listhub-j47: Public git repo directory at /git/ (browse repos)
- listhub-2lg: Git history viewer (commits, diffs)
- listhub-ev1: Clone URL + download button
- listhub-2zb: Auto-push to GitHub + encrypted items (complementary — actual GitHub mirroring)

## Key Principle
Even if the underlying storage isn't a traditional filesystem, the UX should feel like browsing a repo. A generated public-facing version of any user's content should be cloneable/downloadable — this could be the actual bare git repo or a dynamically assembled snapshot filtered to public items only.

## GitHub Niceties to Consider
- Green "Code" button with clone URL (git clone listhub.globalbr.ai/@user.git)
- Download as ZIP button
- File browser view (tree of items)
- Commit history with diffs
- README rendering on repo landing page
- Branch/tag support (future)
- Raw file view
- Contributor list

title: Anonymous/unauthenticated editing UX for directory and public lists
description:
## Problem
Currently the "Edit this page" button on the directory only shows for logged-in users. Logged-out visitors see no edit affordance at all. This is a missed opportunity — if someone lands on the directory and wants to contribute, the path to editing should be obvious and frictionless.

## Immediate Fix (do now)
- Show the "Edit this page" button to ALL visitors (logged in or not)
- If not logged in, clicking it should redirect to login/register with a clear message: "Sign in to edit this page" and a link back after auth
- Include inline instructions for AI agents: "Or use the API: POST /api/v1/auth/register to create an account, then PUT /api/v1/directory/:path to edit"

## Brainstorm: Anonymous Editing Options

### Option A: Require authentication (current, with better UX)
- Edit button visible to everyone, redirects to sign-in
- Lowest risk, full audit trail via git author
- Downside: friction for drive-by contributors

### Option B: Shared "anonymous" account
- Single shared account (e.g., "anonymous") that anyone can use
- All anonymous edits attributed to this account
- Simple but no accountability — vandalism risk
- Could rate-limit by IP

### Option C: Per-session anonymous identity
- Generate a temporary anonymous-XXXX username per session
- Edits are attributed to that session identity
- Slightly better accountability than Option B
- Could require solving a captcha before editing

### Option D: Wiki-style anonymous editing (IP-attributed)
- Allow edits without any account, attribute to IP address in git history
- Most frictionless, like Wikipedia for logged-out users
- Git history shows "Anonymous (192.168.x.x)" as author
- Need moderation tools: recent changes feed, rollback, IP blocking

### Option E: "Suggest edit" (PR-like)
- Anyone can propose edits, but they go into a review queue
- Owner/admins approve or reject
- Best balance of openness and quality control
- More complex to build (needs a pending-edits table or branch-per-edit)

## Trade-offs

| Option | Friction | Accountability | Spam Risk | Complexity |
|--------|----------|---------------|-----------|------------|
| A (auth required) | Medium | Full | Low | Low |
| B (shared anon) | Low | None | High | Low |
| C (session identity) | Low | Partial | Medium | Medium |
| D (wiki-style IP) | None | Partial | High | Medium |
| E (suggest edit) | Low | Full | Low | High |

## For AI Agents
Any option works for agents since they can easily register via the API. The real question is whether we want anonymous HUMAN editing. Agents should always use authenticated API calls regardless.

## Recommendation
Start with Option A (better UX for the redirect) as the immediate fix. Then consider Option E (suggest edit / PR-like) as the long-term solution — it balances openness with quality. Option D (wiki-style) is appealing for maximum contribution but needs moderation tooling first.

## Related
- listhub-z0t: Make lists publicly editable
- listhub-xtc: GitHub-like repo UX epic

title: Add sharing commands - make share/publish/private first-class API operations
Make lists publicly editable

title: Comments or suggested additions on public lists
description: Allow users (or anonymous visitors) to comment on or suggest additions to public lists. Options: (1) Comments section below each item (like GitHub issues), (2) Suggest-an-addition button that creates a pending entry the owner can approve/reject, (3) Both. Consider: anonymous vs auth-required, moderation, notifications to owner.
